text: This is a case of a patient requiring a laparoscopic splenectomy for splenomegaly. The CT scan shows an enlarged spleen. The spleen measured approximately seventeen point four centimeters in maximal dimensions. It has been growing over time.

The surgeon places a 15mm port in the left abdomen and begins mobilizing omental adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall. The inferior pole of the spleen is identified, and the splenic flexure of the colon is mobilized along with the left colon. The spleen is reflected anteriorly, and attachments to the kidney, colon, and spleen are mobilized. The tail of the pancreas is visible posterior to the grasper. Mobilization continues posteriorly to flip the spleen medially, and a 5mm port is inserted into the left flank to retract the spleen laterally and isolate the vessels at the inflopedicle.

The surgeon dissects along the superior edge of the pancreas, revealing the splenic artery and entering the lesser sac. The short gastric vessels are mobilized up to the diaphragmatic hiatus. The posterior attachments of the spleen and the superior pole are being mobilized. The inflow pedicle, containing the splenic vein and artery, is completely isolated and will be transected using a vascular stapler.

The surgeon performs a second firing of the vascular stapler to ensure complete control of the inflow and outflow from the spleen. A grasper is prepared to manage any potential bleeding. Additional posterior and superior attachments of the spleen are mobilized.

The surgeon isolates and takes the final attachments of the spleen, ensuring excellent hemostasis. A large specimen retrieval bag is used to contain the spleen, which is then squeezed into the bag through a fifteen millimeter port site. The spleen is morselized for removal through the port.
